preamble: The shift from the velar stop [k] to the postalveolar fricative [ʃ] is a hallmark of palatalization. It occurs when a velar consonant is pulled forward in the mouth by a following front vowel (like [i] or [e]). This shift usually passes through an intermediate affricate stage [tʃ] before deaffricating to [ʃ].

examples.note: French famously palatalized [k] even before the low vowel [a], creating the 'ch' sound.
The First Palatalization of Velars shifted [k] and [x] to palatal/postalveolar sibilants.

sources: Alkire, T., & Rosen, C. (2010). Romance Languages: A Historical Introduction. Cambridge University Press.
